title: Chapter Nine: Second Skin
content: The Plaza Hotel smells like money — gardenias and old perfume and the particular metallic sweetness of champagne that costs more per glass than most people's rent. I stand at the bar and I don't drink any of it. The champagne flutes pass me by like a river I've chosen not to enter. Instead I press my fingertips against the crystal and feel the cold condensation, the slow sweat of celebration, and I think: *this is what it feels like to show up as a guest.*
Rowan insisted.

Five hundred people fill the grand ballroom — the crème, as Genevieve herself would say, pulling the word long and French until it sounded like a curse. Designers, editors, three sitting senators, the board chairs of every fashion house that matters. The chandeliers throw their Thousand Points of light across the room and I watch them scatter off sequins and cufflinks and the wet rims of glasses. The string quartet plays something French and modern, all tension and resolution, and beneath it the room hums with its own frequency — the sound of five hundred people performing wealth at each other.

I'm wearing my own work. A black dress, deceptively simple from across a room: single dramatic shoulder, clean line to the knee. But up close the architecture reveals itself. Victorian-inspired boning presses against my ribs — I feel each ridge like a second spine, structural and unyielding. Techwear mesh panels run along the sides, breathable and modern, the contrast deliberate. I pressed every seam myself. Not because I don't trust my team — I do, implicitly — but because tonight I needed to feel the stitching under my own hands. I needed to build my own armor.

Suki would have howled at the neckline. She would have said something profane about my collarbones and then ordered us both shots that taste like nail polish remover and we would have laughed until we couldn't breathe. Suki isn't here. Suki is somewhere across the city, alive and loud and not thinking about me at all, and that's exactly how it should be.
I press my palm flat against the boning at my waist. Feel the rigidity. Feel the give.
The quartet pauses. A murmur moves through the room like weather — not a wave, exactly, more like the pressure change before a storm. The grand staircase.

I don't turn. I don't have to. I feel it before I see it, the way you feel a fire at your back — a warmth, a charge, the air rearranging itself around a new center of gravity. Then I turn, because I'm only human, and I've only been human for four hundred years, and I have never in all that time been immune to the sight of my work walking.
Rowan descends the stairs.

The suit is extraordinary. I know this because I made it, because I spent forty-seven hours on the shoulders alone, because I re-cut the lapels three times until they framed his throat like a question worth answering. But seeing it on him — *on him* — in this light, in this room, flanked by chandeliers and five hundred witnesses — I understand for the first time that I made something larger than fabric.

The cut is precise but the drape is alive. The fabric moves like it has a pulse — not floating, not draping, but *breathing*, responding to the angle of his stride, the shift of his shoulders, the way he carries his weight like a man who knows exactly how much space he's allowed to take. The charcoal wool I selected catches the light and gives it back with a slow, deliberate sheen, and the subtle midnight satin on the lapels flashes like something glimpsed through water. Under the chandelier glow, the suit isn't black — it's every shade of dark the world has ever named.

Guests turn. Cameras flash. The murmur becomes a roar — contained, elegant, the specific kind of hysteria that the fashion world calls composure. Someone near me whispers *who* and someone else answers *Ashworth* and a third voice, older, more certain, says: *No — who made that suit?*
I stand at the bar and I watch my work walk into the room and my chest aches with something that isn't pride.
It's possession. It's want.

The hum starts at the base of my skull, resonant and low, the way it always does when Rowan is near and I'm not fighting it. I've been fighting it for months. Tonight the fight feels ceremonial, a formality I'm maintaining out of habit. Because the suit is on his body and my seams are holding and every person in this room is looking at him and seeing — *me*. My hand. My vision. My hunger stitched into shoulder seams and vent vents and a silhouette that declares: *this is what he looks like when someone sees him clearly.*
And the worst part — the most dangerous part — is that I want to walk up to every single one of them and say: *Yes. You're welcome. Now look away.*
I don't. I stay at the bar. I press my fingertips to the champagne flute and feel the cold and the wet and I wait.
He finds me.

Not by accident. Not by the wandering drift of a room in circulation. He moves through the throng like a man who just found what he was looking for — deliberate, certain, uncrowded by the people who step aside because something in his trajectory tells them to. The suit fits him like gravity. Like the laws of physics were drafted specifically so that wool could fall across those shoulders at exactly that angle.
He stops in front of me.

Close. Closer than convention. Close enough that I catch his cologne — oud, bergamot, and beneath it something older, something that doesn't come from any bottle. Something like woodsmoke and cold stone and the particular green smell of English rain, and I know — I *know* — that I've smelled that base note before, in a different century, in a different body, in a room where the candles guttered and he turned to me and said my name and it wasn't Orlando but it was still *me*.
The hum flares. Bright and urgent, a struck bell inside my skull.
"You made this," he says. Not a question. His voice is quiet and it carries anyway, the way certain sounds cut through rooms — the way the right frequency can shatter glass.

"I stitched it," I say, because I don't know how to tell the truth yet. Because *I stitched it* is a fact, and *I made this for you because I have been making things for you since before either of us understood why* is the kind of confession that ends in catastrophe.
He holds out his hand.

The emerald ring catches the chandelier light and throws it back in fragments — green and gold and that particular brilliance that only comes from a stone formed under impossible pressure. He's worn it every time I've seen him. I've never asked about it. Some questions are too specific to survive the asking.
This time the hand isn't a greeting. It's an invitation.

I look at his fingers — long, certain, the knuckles slightly rough from something that isn't desk work. I look at the ring. I think about every hand I've taken in four hundred years. The suitors in Amsterdam who thought my silence was modesty. The lovers in London who mistook my attention for passion. The one — the only one — in a drafty parish church who turned to me with hazel eyes and said *I think I've known you before* and I ran because I was terrified and young and hadn't yet learned that running from him only means arriving by a different route.
I take his hand.
His grip is warm and certain. The hum flares — not a warning anymore, or maybe it always was a warning and I've stopped caring about the distinction. I don't let go.
He leads me to the dance floor.

The quartet has shifted — something slower now, something that aches, a melody built on half-steps and unresolved sevenths that make the chest tighten before the brain catches up. Rowan turns to face me. His left hand finds my waist. His right holds my hand, and his palm is warm through the mesh panel at my side, and I feel the boning in my dress press against my ribs and I feel his fingers press against the boning and for a disorienting moment I can't tell where the structure ends and his touch begins.
He leads.

I haven't danced with anyone in forty years. My body remembers anyway — it remembers everything, the waltzes in Vienna when the candles were whale-fat and the floors were swept straw, the country dances in Hampshire where the music was fiddle and foot and the whole room moved in patterns older than the house, the club nights in the Nineties when the bass hit like a heart attack and we danced in packs, territorial and gleeful, sweating out the decade's excess. My body carries every rhythm I've ever learned.
I follow him like I was made for his lead.

Because in some way I was. I've been following his soul for four hundred years. Through names and cities and the slow cataclysm of change, through the times I found him and the times I lost him and the times I stood in a room just like this one and watched him turn toward me with that particular confusion — the face of a man trying to remember a dream.

We move together. The room recedes. The chandeliers blur. The five hundred witnesses become set dressing — a forest of dark shapes and glittering edges that exists only to make this moment private by contrast, the way a crowded room can be the most intimate space in the world when you stop seeing anyone in it except the person whose hand is on your waist.

The boning presses. His hand presses back. The counterpressure is precise, controlled — he's not holding me so much as conversing with the architecture of the dress, reading the boning through the mesh like braille, finding the places where the structure gives and the places where it doesn't.
"You look like someone I've been trying to remember my whole life," he whispers.

The words hit me like a hand across the face. My breath catches — a small, ugly sound, half gasp and half grief. The hum screams. Four hundred years of *I know you* and *have we met* and *you seem familiar* and every time it tears something open that I've spent decades stitching shut.
I almost say: *You have.*
I almost say: *In 1634 in a church in Devon you turned to me and said the same thing and I was so frightened I didn't speak to you again for sixty years.*
I almost say: *I have loved you in every shape you've worn and I will love you in every shape you'll wear and I am so tired of pretending this is new.*
Instead I say: "Maybe you knew me in a different life."
He pulls me closer. The boning groans. His cologne — oud, bergamot, rain and smoke and old stone — fills the narrow space between us until I'm breathing him instead of air.
"Maybe I did," he says, and his voice is warm and low and certain, the voice of a man who isn't afraid of the impossible — who maybe, in some room of himself he hasn't opened yet, already knows.

We turn. The quartet swells. The chandeliers scatter their light across his shoulders and the suit holds — my seams, my pressing, my forty-seven hours on those shoulders — and I think: *this is what it feels like to be seen.* Not the suit. Not the work. *Me.* The obscene, impossible, devastating thing of being recognized.
Across the room — a flash of white. A phone.
Maren Voss.

She stands near the far column, half-shadowed, her phone held at an angle that pretends to be casual and isn't. She's photographing us. Not the room, not the suit, not the spectacle of Genevieve Ashford's farewell — *us.* Orlando and Rowan. Dancing too close. Looking at each other like the rest of the room has been erased.
She lowers the phone. Her eyes narrow.

I watch her watching me and something shifts in her expression — not recognition, not the heat of *I know who you are.* Something worse. The recognition of a pattern. The professional cataloguing of a bone structure, a posture, a way of holding the jaw. I've seen that look before, in archivists and historians and the sort of people who collect daguerreotypes and notice when a woman in an 1890 photograph has the exact same zygomatic arch as a woman in a 2026 gala dress.
She raises the phone again. This time it points at my face.

The hum spikes — a shriek, a siren, the sound of a door being forced — and in the white noise I hear the always-warning, the ancient imperative: *run, hide, vanish, start again.* Four hundred years of escape plans activate in my blood like antibodies. I could be gone by midnight. New name, new city, new body, new century. I've done it before. I know the choreography of disappearance.
But Rowan is saying my name.
"Orlando."

His voice is warm and low and close enough that I feel it more than hear it — the vibration of it in his chest where it presses against the boning at my ribs, a frequency that travels through structure and mesh and armor until it reaches the thing underneath, the thing I keep in a locked room inside myself, the thing that has never stopped being his.
I don't look at Maren Voss.

I look at Rowan. At his face — the same face, the same eyes, the same particular angle of jaw that I first saw across a room that smelled like tallow and pine, four hundred years and thirty-seven names ago. He's looking at me like I'm a door he's been knocking on for a lifetime. Like he can hear something on the other side.

The hum is so loud it's almost silence — a pitch beyond hearing, a pressure in my skull that feels like the atmosphere before a lightning strike. Every cell in my body is saying *careful* and *stop* and *you know how this ends.*
I don't stop.
I am drowning in the most dangerous thing in the world: the feeling of being known.
I'm in love. I've been in love for four hundred years. And every time I let myself feel it, the hum gets louder.
